Y  Controllability and observability of dynamical networks
S have been extensively studied in the controls-engineering
.
s community [1]–[10]. One key question that has found at-
s
tention recently is how to design controls  in a network
e
e  to shape observability and controllability while maintaining
[
  network performance. Such design problems are particularly
 
relevant in contexts where multiple stakeholders (including
1
v adversaries) have access  to the network’s dynamics,  and
8 have conflicting objectives in modulating the dynamics. For
2 example, increasing concern about cyber-attacks in the power
3
grid has led to significant  interest  in designing wide-area
3
control systems that prevent adversaries from estimating or
1
.
manipulating the dynamics [11]. Similarly, controller designs
2
for multi-vehicle systems  require security from intruders
0
5 probing a subset of vehicles to infer the system’s state [12].
2  In this regard, observability-blocking has found usefulness
:
in protecting privacy in dynamical networks [13].
v
i  Based on this motivation,  in our previous work we ex-
X plored design of feedback controls in a linear synchronization
r
network to block observability/controllablity at a remote set
a
of nodes, while maintaining all  the eigenvalues and most
of the eigenvectors [14]–[16]. However, the synchronization
model considered in our earlier works has a limitation that
each node is associated with only a scalar state. In contrast,
double-integrator networks (DIN) and higher-order integrator
network dynamics, which are widely used to model and con-
trol high-fidelity multi-agent systems such as robotic motion
control and tracking in unmanned vehicle networks, consider
network nodes with multiple states [17], [18]. Specifically,
DIN is extensively used as a network model for the formation

control of unmanned vehicle networks, among many other
applications [12], [17]. Therefore, in this work we focus on
the design of observability blocking controls for DIN model.
We also study generalization of our design for higher order
integrator networks.
Our main focus of this study is to utilize the dynamics
and topological structure of an integrator network to develop
a design algorithm that  can prevent observability of  the
measurements obtained from a given set of nodes. Our main
contributions for this paper are the following:
1) An algorithm that blocks observability of a set of m
nodes in DIN is presented using state-feedback controls at
another set of q = m + 2 nodes. The algorithm builds
upon the design from our earlier work [16] and maintains
all  the eigenvalues and most of  the eigenvectors. Unlike
previous work,  the algorithm leverages a special property
of eigenvectors in a DIN model  to limit  the actuation to
q = m+ 2.
2) A sparser design scheme is then presented that exploits
the topological structure of the DIN model. Similar to our
previous work, we show that the number of actuation can be
reduced by blocking observability at cutsets separating the
actuation and measurement  locations. However,  the condi-
tions required for this design in the context of DIN differ
significantly from those established in our earlier work.
3) We then extend our results for N-th order integrator net-
works. The number of actuation nodes required for blocking
observability remains the same as in the DIN case.
The paper is organized as follows: In section II, we formu-
late the observability-blocking controller design problem for
DIN. We present our main results on observability-blocking
controls in Section III for both DIN and N-th order integrator
networks. Section IV is devoted to a numerical example, and
in Section V we draw conclusions.

In this study, we first focus on a double-integrator network
(DIN) model which will allow us develop design principles
for higher order integrator network models. Specifically, here
we consider a DIN with n nodes labeled as 1, 2, · · · , n. Each
node i is associated with two scalar states denoted as si and
s˙i. In the context of a vehicle network, si and s˙i correspond
to the position and velocity of the vehicle i. The interactions
between different nodes in the DIN are represented by a
directed graph G(V, E), where V contains n nodes/vertices
and E ⊂ V × V contains a collection of directed edges
specified as an ordered pair of nodes. We assume the graph is
strongly connected and use N(i) to denote the set of nodes

In this section, we present our results for the design of
observability-blocking controls. First, we develop a design
algorithm for DIN requiring q = m + 2 actuation nodes.
Then, we provide a sparser design that utilizes the topo-
logical structure of DIN to reduce the required number of
actuation nodes. Finally, we extend our results to encompass
N-th order integrator networks.
A. General Design of Observability-Blocking Controller for
DIN
In this section, we present a general algorithm for design-
ing a state-feedback controller that makes the pair (C, (A+
BF)) unobservable while preserving much of  the open-
loop eigenstructure. The design relies on the eigenstructure
assignment method, where a selected eigenvector will be
modified to enforce unobservability. We stress that the algo-
rithm presented here is built upon our previous algorithm (see
Algorithm 1 in [16]). Our main contribution for this section
is that we provide a generalization of the design algorithm
given in [16] so that it can be applied to a DIN while keeping
the required actuation the same as before.
For the simplicity of our development, we assume eigen-
values are non-defective (i.e. algebraic and geometric mul-
tiplicities of each eigenvalue are same). However, the result
also applies for non-defective eigenvalues as discussed later.
We  label  the eigenvalues of A as λ1, λ2, . . . , λ2n,  and
note that corresponding eigenvectors v1, v2, . . . , v2n can be
C2n
found which are linearly independent and span  . In the
ensuing development, for notational convenience and without
loss of generality, the last m nodes (nodes n−m+1, . . . , n)
are assumed to be the measurement nodes. Hence, Cˆ =
T
[en−m+1 en−m+2  · · · en]  .
The design algorithm is based on selecting one of  the
eigenvalues of A and its associated eigenvector,  say λp
and vp where p ∈ {1, 2, . . . , 2n}. A feedback controller
is constructed using eigenstructure assignment method, such
that  the eigenvector vp is modified to a vector vˆp whose
entries corresponding to the measurement nodes i.e. n−m+

1, n −m+ 2, · · · , n and 2n −m+ 1, 2n −m+ 2, · · · , 2n
entries are all zeros (v¯p is also modified commensurately
if  the selected λp  is complex).  In this way, based on the
Popov-Belevitch-Hautus (PBH) test [20], the mode λp of the
system dynamics is made unobservable since Cvˆp = 0. The
design preserves most of the remaining eigenvectors and all
the eigenvalues to maintain the open-loop eigenstructure as
much as possible.
In our previous study [16], we showed that we require two
1

Thus we could modify 2m entries of vp (i.e. n−m+1, n−
m+ 2, · · · , n and 2n−m+ 1, 2n−m+ 2, · · · , 2n entries)
2
to become zero by using q = 2m + 2 actuation nodes .
However,  it  is possible  to achieve observability-blocking
through q = m + 2 actuation nodes,  by exploiting the
dynamics of DIN. To do so, we now present a result which
establishes a relationship among entries of an eigenvector of
the closed-loop DIN model.
Lemma 1: Consider the closed-loop dynamics of  the DIN
model given by (5). Suppose λp and vˆp are the eigenvalue
and eigenvector pair of (A+BF). Then the j-th and (j+n)th
entries of  the eigenvector, denoted as vˆp,j and vˆp,j+n, are
related by: vˆp,j+n = λp vˆp,j , where j = 1, 2, · · · , n.
Proof:
To establish the relationship among entries of vˆp, we

From the partition of vˆp and eqn. (7),  it is immediate that

□
Note that Lemma 1 also holds for the open-loop dynamics
of DIN, as seen by setting F = 0. Now, from Lemma 1 we
see that if vˆp,j = 0 then vˆp,j+n = 0, where j = 1, 2, · · · , n.
Thus,  if we enforce zero to n − m + 1, n − m + 2, · · · , n
entries of vˆp, then the 2n−m+1, 2n−m+2, · · · , 2n entries
will also become zero. Therefore, we can adopt Algorithm
1 of [16] and update it to enforce observability to our DIN
model (5) by using only q = m + 2 actuation nodes. The
adapted algorithm for blocking observability in DIN based
on [16] is presented in the appendix. We formalize the result
of the algorithm in the following theorem.
1
If all the eigenvalues are real, then we would need one more actuation.
2
If all the eigenvalues are real, then we would need q = 2m+1 actuation
nodes.

Theorem 1: Consider the DIN model (5). Assume that: (1)
3
the eigenvalues of A are non-defective , (2) q ≥ m+2, and
(3) the pair (A, B) is controllable.

Then the gain matrix F
of the state feedback controller can be designed to block ob-
servability of the model, i.e. to make the pair (C, (A+BF))
unobservable. Specifically,  the controller can be designed
via Algorithm 1 so that any selected non-zero open-loop
eigenvalue λp becomes unobservable. Furthermore, all open-
loop eigenvalues and the open-loop eigenvectors in the set
V0 ∩ V1, as defined in Algorithm 1, are maintained in the
closed-loop model.
Proof:
Given that conditions (1)-(3) are satisfied, Theorem 1 of
[16] guarantees that the algorithm is always able to enforce
m entries of vˆp  to become zero, while maintaining all
open-loop eigenvalues and open-loop eigenvectors of the set
V0 ∩ V1. Now, since we take N4(λp) hp = 0 in Step 3, the
algorithm enforces zero to the n−m+1, n−m+2, · · · , n
entries of vˆp. However, Lemma 1 implies that 2n − m +
1, 2n − m + 2, · · · , 2n entries of vˆp will  also be zero.
Therefore, according to the (PBH) test, the eigenvalue λp of
the system dynamics is made unobservable since Cvˆp = 0.
□
According to [16], we note that when all the eigenvalues
are real, only q = m+1 actuation nodes are needed. We refer
readers to [16] for important remarks on Algorithm 1. We
make the following additional remarks regarding Algorithm
1 presented here:
1) Algorithm 1 and Theorem 1 as presented here can
be applied to the case when A has defective eigenvalues.
Reference [16] outlines the modifications needed for the case
of defective eigenvalues, which involves the modification of
generalized eigenvectors. The modified algorithm of  [16]
can be adjusted following the same adjustment made  in
Algorithm 1 here to enforce observability in a DIN model.
We omit further discussion on this for the sake of simplicity
and to avoid repetition.
2) In Step 5, we could choose hp so that N6(λp) hp = 0.
In that case,  the algorithm will directly enforce 2n − m +
1, 2n − m + 2, · · · , 2n entries of vˆp to become zero. But
according  to Lemma 1, n − m + 1, n − m + 2, · · · , n
entries of vˆp will also become zero given that λp ̸= 0.
Thus, unobservability will also be ensured with an additional
condition that the selected eigenvalue is nonzero in Step 1
i.e. λp ̸= 0.
3) The result presented here does not depend on the
s  s˙
Laplacian forms of L and L . Thus, the algorithm can be
applied to any DIN which does not achieve synchronization
or consensus for a zero input response.
B. Sparser Observability-Blocking Using Network Cutsets
In this section, we show that by utilizing the topological
structure of DIN, we can induce unobservability with a
3
Reference [16] initially assumed distinct eigenvalues for development of
Theorem 1, but later it was shown that Theorem 1 applies for non-defective
eigenvalues also. We present  this result for non-defective case as DIN is
known to have repeated eigenvalues.

smaller set of actuation nodes than the q = m + 2 action
nodes. This sparser design follows  from the  insight  that
blocking observability at the nodes associated with a cutset of
the network graph using actuation in one partition can serve
to block observability at all nodes associated with the other
partition. We formalize this result first, and then provide our
sparser observability-blocking design.
For the convenience of our development, as we did in [14],
[16], here we define two network models which have differ-
ent measurement paradigms. We consider a base network
model as defined in Section II, with defined state, input, and
output matrices for the model as A, B, and C. A second
model is then defined (with the same state dynamics and ac-
tuation nodes), but with a distinct measurement model. This
measurement model considers a node-cutset of the graph G
that partitions the actuation and measurement nodes in the
base model. As shown in Fig. 1,  the cutset Vcut separates
the graph into two partitions V1 and V2 in such a way that
V1 does not  include any measurement nodes and V2 does
not include any actuation nodes. We aptly name this second
model the cutset-measurement network model. We stress that
the cutset may include the actuation or measurement nodes
of the base model as shown in Fig. 1, and hence a cutset
always exists such that |Vcut| ≤ m where |Vcut| represents
the cardinality of Vcut. In the cutset-measurement network
model, the measurement nodes are defined as the nodes of
this cutset. Therefore,  the corresponding output matrix is

The  following  lemma gives  a condition under which
observability-blocking in the cutset-measurement network
model implies observability-blocking in the base model. This
lemma will then be used to obtain a sparser observability-
blocking controller design.

associated cutset-measurement network model. Suppose the
gain matrix F of  the state feedback controller is designed
to block observability in the cutset-measurement network
model, in such a way that the pair (Cˆ , (A+ BF)) has an

this feedback controller also serves to block observability in

also has an unobservable eigenvalue at λp.

Note,  the condition given  in Lemma 2  is completely
different than what we had in our earlier work [16]. Now,
Lemma 2 serves as a basis for our algorithm for sparser
observability-blocking control. The idea is to first use the
algorithm from Section III-A to design an observability-
blocking controller  for  the  cutset-measurement  network
model, whereupon Lemma 2 can be leveraged to guarantee
that observability is blocked in the base model also. The
overall design procedure can be described as follows: First,
a small-cardinality vertex-cutset Vcut separating the measure-
ment nodes from the actuation nodes in the network graph
is chosen, and the associated cutset-measurement network
model  is formed. Then,  in accordance with the algorithm
in Section III-A, an eigenvalue λp of A is chosen, whose
eigenvector will be modified to block observability. However,
an additional condition is  imposed on the selection,  that
2  s  s˙
λp  is not an eigenvalue of Lg = −(LV2V2 + λpLV2V2).
Thereupon, the algorithm presented in Section III-A can be
applied to find the controller that makes the eigenvalue at
λp unobservable in the cutset-measurement network model
using |Vcut| + 2 actuation nodes. From Lemma 2,  it  is
immediate that the same controller blocks observability in the
base network model. In this way an observability-blocking
controller  is found that  requires only |Vcut| + 2 actuation
nodes. Further,  this controller maintains all of  the open-
loop eigenvalues, according to Theorem 1. We formalize this
design in the following theorem.
Theorem 2: Consider the network model (3), and say that a
separating vertex-cutset Vcut has been found. Assume that:
1) q ≥ |Vcut| + 2, 2) the graph G of the network model is
strongly connected and has positive edge-weights, and 3) the
pair (A, B) is controllable.

Then the gain matrix F of state
feedback controller can be designed to block observability,
i.e. to make the pair (C, (A+BF)) unobservable. Further-
more, all  the open-loop eigenvalues are maintained in the
closed-loop system for the design.
Proof:
For  the proof, all we need to show is that  there exists
R2n×2n  2
an eigenvalue λp of A ∈  such that λp is not an
eigenvalue of Lg. To show that, we choose a fixed eigenvalue
s
of A, which is 0. Now, choosing λp = 0, we get Lg = −L22.
Since the network graph G is strongly connected and the
edge-weights of the graph are assumed to be positive, then
s
the grounded Laplacian L22 has all of its eigenvalues in the
open right half plane (see Theorem 1 of [21]). Hence, Lg
has all of  its eigenvalues in the open left half plane and
cannot have a zero eigenvalue. Therefore, we can conclude

that there always exists an eigenvalue λp to choose so that
the condition mentioned in Lemma 2 is satisfied.
□
We can see that Theorem 2 provides an appealing ap-
proach for enforcing unobservability in a sparse DIN net-
work with a small cutset. Many real-world networks, such
as power-grids,  transportation, and scale-free networks, are
sparse and have small-cardinality cutsets [22]. In such net-
works, sparser design is useful to block observability using
only a few actuator nodes. Note, when all  the eigenvalues
of A are real, we can use q = |Vcut| + 1 actuation nodes
in our design. We further remark that the results presented
s  s˙
here do not  rely on the Laplacian forms of L  and L
matrices, except when there is need for the existence of a
zero eigenvalue in the proof of Theorem 2. Given that the
2
selected eigenvalue satisfies the technical criterion: λp is not
an eigenvalue of matrix Lg, the sparser design holds for any
s  s˙
form of L and L matrices.
C. Extension to N-th Order Integrator Network
Following the approach presented in this paper, we can
easily extend our results for an N-th order integrator network
where N ∈ N.  In this section we present  the results and
briefly discuss their development. We omit  formal proofs
as these can be easily derived using the same arguments
presented in previous two sections of this paper.
In  an N-th  order  integrator  network,  each  node
i  is  associated  with  N  states,  which  can  be
N−1
represented  as  si(t), s˙i(t), s¨i(t), · · · , si  (t).  Here,
N−1
si  (t)  denotes  the  (N − 1)-th  time-derivative  of
si(t).  The  network  states  are  defined  as  x(t)  =
N−1  N−1  T
[s1(t)  · · ·  sn(t)  · · ·  s1  (t)  · · ·  sn  (t)]  .  We
again enhance this network model by capturing a set of
actuation nodes and a set of measurement nodes,  similar
to Section II. The N-th order integrator network dynamics
with actuation and measurement included, is then given by
the following state space model:

where Bˆ and Cˆ are defined as before. Therefore, the open-

written as (3) and (5) respectively. Again, our objective is to
design a state-feedback controller, defined by the gain matrix
F, to enforce that the pair (C, (A+BF)) is unobservable,
while maintaining as much of the open-loop eigenstructure
as possible..

numerical example
In  this  section, we present  a numerical  example  that
verifies our theoretical findings. We adopt the same example
graph used in [16],  but now considering DIN dynamics
for  the nodes. Specifically,  an 11 node network with an
undirected graph G is used as an example, as shown in Fig.
s  s˙
2. The edge weights for G in the Laplacians L  and L
are assigned random positive numbers. The measurement
nodes are assumed to be {6, 8, 9, 11}. We  identify from
Fig. 2 that node 5 is a potential  single-node cutset  that
results in the graph’s measurement nodes coalesced into one
partition. Thus, from Theorems 1 and 2, we should be able
to build state feedback controllers at any three nodes from
{1, 2, 3, 4, 10} to block observability at node 5, and hence at
the measurement nodes. However, since all the eigenvalues
of A are real, q = 2 is sufficient to block observability by
using our algorithm. We choose nodes 1 and 10 for actuation.
To build the controller, we select one eigenvalue and its
associated eigenvector of the matrix A. Specifically we select
λp = −1.0099 whose corresponding eigenvector  is vp =
[0.0305, 0.1342, −0.1163, −0.0948, 0.0118, 0.4642, −0.4413,
−0.0857, 0.1786, −0.0594, −0.0218, −0.0308, −0.1355,
0.1174, 0.0958, −0.0119, −0.4688, 0.4457, 0.0865, −0.1803,

A design algorithm has been developed for observability-
blocking controls  in a double  integrator network (DIN).
The topological structure of DIN has then been exploited
to reduce the number of actuation nodes needed to block
observability. We have generalized the observability-blocking
design for N-th order  integrator networks. A numerical
example has been presented to verify our findings. One
interesting future direction for this study includes developing
design of observability-blocking controls for more complex
networks, such as nonlinear dynamical networks.
ACKNOWLEDGMENT
We thank Prof. Sandip Roy at Texas A&M University for
his valuable suggestions and insights regarding this work.

steps under Sub-Algorithm 2.
